text: Code refactoring is an essential practice for keeping software healthy. It means improving the internal structure of code without changing how it behaves. By making small, careful changes, developers can increase readability, making the code easier to understand and modify later. For example, simplifying complex loops, renaming unclear variables, or breaking long functions into smaller ones are common refactoring steps. This process helps reduce technical debt—the extra work created by messy code over time. While refactoring doesn't add new features, it prevents future problems and keeps the project manageable. Regular refactoring is like cleaning a room; it takes effort now but saves time and frustration in the long run.
